target.title: fix(ui): make session history accessible in picker and sidebar
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95601.

VoiceOver and keyboard users can open the Control UI chat session picker today, but the history experience was still split across partial slices: unreadable scoped session keys when labels are missing, no direct rename action in the chat picker, and sidebar recent sessions that did not expose separate switch/reopen and rename controls with clear accessible names.

This PR makes both the chat session picker and the sidebar recent-session history expose human-readable titles, timestamps, active-state text, switch/reopen controls, and rename controls through standard keyboard-focusable controls.

## Scope / Overlap Decision

This branch is intentionally a combined Control UI session-history accessibility implementation. It includes the derived-title loading/display scope that overlaps the narrower sidebar/picker title work in #94544, then extends that baseline with switch/reopen semantics, rename controls, sidebar coverage, shared persisted-label handling, and exact-head browser proof.

Maintainers can treat this PR as the candidate canonical fix for #95601 if they prefer one combined picker + sidebar accessibility branch. If maintainers prefer a narrower derived-title-only UX first, #94544 can still be considered separately and this PR can remain paused. This PR does not close or mutate sibling PRs automatically.

## Summary

- requests `includeDerivedTitles` for chat/recent-session loads so unlabeled conversations get readable derived titles instead of raw session keys
- exposes each picker row as a keyboard-reachable switch action with a readable accessible name containing title, timestamp metadata, and active-state text
- adds a keyboard-reachable rename action beside each picker/sidebar row, reusing one shared `promptAndRenameChatSession` helper around `sessions.patch`
- preserves chat-scoped refresh parameters, including `includeDerivedTitles`, after label changes
- updates the sidebar recent-session list so each row exposes separate switch/reopen and rename controls with title, timestamp, and active-state announcements
- keeps the existing New Session control reachable with its existing `aria-label`
- updates generated locale bundles after adding the new English picker/sidebar action strings

## Accessibility Notes

The picker now uses a regular list of session rows rather than an ARIA listbox. Each row contains separate, focusable controls for:

- switching/reopening the session (`Switch to session: <title> · <timestamp> · Active` when current)
- renaming the session (`Rename session: <title> · <timestamp> · Active` when current)

The sidebar recent-session history follows the same pattern: each row is a list item with a link-style switch/reopen control plus a separate rename button. The active session uses `aria-current="page"` and also includes explicit `Active` text in the accessible name.

This avoids mixing ARIA `option` semantics with sibling action buttons while preserving keyboard navigation through standard links/buttons.

## Session Label Write Path

The picker and sidebar both route through `promptAndRenameChatSession`, which is the single UI helper that:

- checks connection/client availability
- prompts with the existing label or fallback display title
- converts blank input to `{ label: null }`
- calls `sessions.patch` with chat-scoped refresh options and `includeDerivedTitles`

This keeps the persisted label behavior centralized even though two UI entry points expose the same rename action.

## Evidence

- Added jsdom coverage that verifies derived titles render in the chat picker and the switch control accessible name includes title, timestamp metadata, and active-state text.
- Added jsdom coverage that verifies each picker row exposes a rename control and persists the new label through the shared rename helper while retaining chat-scoped refresh parameters.
- Added jsdom coverage that verifies sidebar recent sessions expose New Session, readable title, timestamp, active state, switch/reopen, and rename accessible controls.
- Added jsdom coverage that verifies sidebar recent-session rename uses the shared helper with chat-scoped refresh parameters and derived-title loading preserved.
- Added focused helper coverage for rename, blank-label clearing, cancelled prompt behavior, and shared `sessions.patch` arguments.
- Added controller coverage for forwarding `includeDerivedTitles` and for patch refresh override preservation.
- Ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat/session-rename.test.ts ui/src/ui/navigation.browser.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts`:
  - `ui/src/ui/navigation.browser.test.ts`: 14 passed
  - `ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.test.ts`: 81 passed
  - `ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts`: 22 passed
  - `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`: 121 passed
  - `ui/src/ui/chat/session-rename.test.ts`: 3 passed
- Ran targeted `corepack pnpm format:check` on changed UI files: passed
- Ran targeted `OPENCLAW_OXLINT_SHARDS_SERIAL=1 node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json ...` on changed UI files: passed
- Ran `git diff --check`: passed

## Real Behavior Proof

- Exact-head browser proof run: <https://github.com/snowzlmbot/openclaw/actions/runs/28081574322>
- Browser artifact: `control-ui-session-history-proof-a7faf9d8ccab43c68d5af689d9b2fc62e6bafe2d`
- The proof checks out exact PR head `a7faf9d8ccab43c68d5af689d9b2fc62e6bafe2d`, starts the Control UI mock dev server, captures a screenshot (`control-ui-session-history.png`), and uploads DOM assertions (`control-ui-session-history-proof.json`).
- Verified DOM summary from the artifact:
  - New Session: `aria-label="New session"`
  - Sidebar switch/reopen: `Switch to session: Alpha planning · 33d ago · Active`
  - Sidebar rename: `Rename session: Alpha planning · 33d ago · Active`
  - Picker switch/reopen: `Switch to session: Alpha planning · openai/gpt-5.5 · 5/22/2026, 8:59:59 AM · Active`
  - Picker rename: `Rename session: Alpha planning · openai/gpt-5.5 · 5/22/2026, 8:59:59 AM · Active`
- All browser proof assertions passed for title readability, active-state announcement, switch/reopen controls, and rename controls in both picker and sidebar.

## macOS VoiceOver Attempt

- Same run: <https://github.com/snowzlmbot/openclaw/actions/runs/28081574322>
- VoiceOver artifact: `control-ui-session-history-voiceover-a7faf9d8ccab43c68d5af689d9b2fc62e6bafe2d`
- Runner: macOS 15.7.7 (`24G720`) with `/private/var/db/Accessibility/.VoiceOverAppleScriptEnabled` present.
- Result: `blocked`. After launching VoiceOver with `VoiceOverStarter`, `osascript` timed out while reading `Application("VoiceOver").lastPhrase.content()`. The artifact includes `voiceover-permission-diagnostics.txt`, `control-ui-session-history-voiceover-proof.json`, `control-ui-session-history-voiceover-phrases.txt`, and `control-ui-session-history-voiceover-target.png`.
- This means the stricter live VoiceOver speech transcript was attempted but is currently blocked by runner VoiceOver automation behavior. The exact-head browser proof still verifies the accessible names and states that VoiceOver should consume.

## Not Tested

- A successful live macOS VoiceOver speech transcript is not available yet because the GitHub-hosted macOS runner timed out when querying VoiceOver `lastPhrase` after startup, even though the VoiceOver AppleScript marker exists. If maintainers require this final confidence layer, it should be captured on a macOS environment where VoiceOver AppleScript automation is fully responsive.

target.title: fix(cron): classify recovered agentTurn as non-fatal when finalAssistantVisibleText exists
target.body: ## Summary Cron `agentTurn` runs that encounter a transient tool error mid-run and recover with a successful `finalAssistantVisibleText` are now classified as `ok` instead of `error`, ensuring the success announce delivery is dispatched. - **Problem**: `resolveCronPayloadOutcome` classified runs as fatal when no existing recovery gate could fire. The four gates all failed because the raw exec error payload lacked the required metadata markers (`nonTerminalToolErrorWarning`), text prefixes (`⚠️ 🛠️`), or deliverable post-error payloads (`hasSuccessfulPayloadAfterLastError`). The agent's `finalAssistantVisibleText` — which proved recovery — was never consulted. - **Solution**: Add `hasRecoveredByFinalAnswer` gate that treats a non-empty `finalAssistantVisibleText` as recovery proof when the session completed without a run-level error or fatal failure signal. - **What changed**: `src/cron/isolated-agent/helpers.ts` (+17/-2: new `hasRecoveredByFinalAnswer` gate + delivery coupling for false-policy channels), `src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts` (+83/-12: 2 updated tests + 4 new tests including Feishu/Slack channel regression) - **What did NOT change**: Run-level error handling, `fatalForCron` failure signal precedence, `dispatchCronDelivery` call site, channel output policy resolver, SDK surface, config, or migration paths. ## What Problem This Solves Cron `agentTurn` runs with `delivery.mode: "announce"` were silently failing every night when the agent hit a transient tool error mid-run (e.g. `exec` rejecting shell redirect syntax like `>`, `2>&1`), retried successfully, completed all remaining steps, and emitted a full final report. The cron runner misclassified the recovered run as `status: "error"` because no existing recovery gate consulted `finalAssistantVisibleText` — the agent runtime's own signal of successful completion. The operator received a failure notification synthesized from the already-recovered tool error instead of the actual report. ## Change Type & Scope ### Change Type - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ### Scope - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue - Related to #96255 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Motivation A cron `agentTurn` with `delivery.mode: "announce"` was silently failing every night for 6 consecutive days. The agent hit a transient `exec` tool error (shell redirect syntax rejected), retried successfully, completed 60+ further tool calls, and emitted a full final report — but the operator received no report at all. The cron run log showed `status=error` with a synthesized failure notification built from the already-recovered tool error. This affects any cron whose agent habitually probes with shell-isms the `exec` tool rejects (`>`, `2>&1`, `;`, quoted python one-liners) and then retries — a common recovery pattern across multiple model providers. ## Review Contract ```text Ref: #96255 / PR #TBD Surface: cron / isolated-agent outcome classification Bug: Recovered tool errors misclassified as fatal when finalAssistantVisibleText proves recovery Cause: hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload lacked a gate consulting finalAssistantVisibleText; all four existing gates require specific payload markers or deliverability that plain exec errors don't carry (src/cron/isolated-agent/helpers.ts:299-304) 置信度: clear Provenance: introduced by: hasRecoveredToolWarning gate (PR #TBD) — only covers ⚠️ 🛠️ prefixed errors made visible by: agent model behavior change — more frequent plain exec retry patterns 置信度: likely Best fix: Yes. Adding a finalAssistantVisibleText-based gate is the minimal correct fix because finalAssistantVisibleText is the agent runtime's canonical signal of completion. The alternative (broadening isSuccessfulCronPayload) would change downstream delivery selection behavior and doesn't cover cases where no post-error reply payloads exist. The second commit couples the recovery gate with delivery-payload selection so finalAssistantVisibleText is also dispatched on Feishu/Slack channels where preferFinalAssistantVisibleText is false. Refactor: No — the existing gate structure already handles structured/content-specific recovery patterns; this adds the missing general case and ensures delivery consistency. Proof: 终端输出 (5 real scenarios via npx tsx, 89 unit tests across 3 files) Risk: Low. The hasErrorPayload guard on the delivery override ensures normal success runs without errors are unaffected. The !runLevelError and !fatalForCron guards prevent genuine fatal errors from being downgraded. ``` ## Real Behavior Proof **Behavior addressed**: Cron `agentTurn` with recovered tool error + `finalAssistantVisibleText` → misclassified as `error`, delivery skipped. After fix: classification corrected to `ok`, final report delivered on all channels including Feishu/Slack (where `preferFinalAssistantVisibleText` is false). **Real environment tested**: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node 24.13.1, branch `fix/cron-recovered-final-answer-96255`, built with `pnpm build` (883s, clean exit) **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ```bash # Build the project pnpm build # Unit tests pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts --run pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts --run pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.payload-fallbacks.test.ts --run # Real behavior proof: exercise resolveCronPayloadOutcome with 5 real scenarios npx tsx scripts/proof-96255.mjs ``` **Evidence after fix**: ```console $ npx tsx scripts/proof-96255.mjs ======================================================================== SCENARIO: #96255: exec error → agent recovers → full final report (preferFinalAssistantVisibleText=true) ======================================================================== Inputs: payloads: [{"text":"Working on the daily query…"},{"text":"{\"ok\":false,\"error\":\"unknown arg: >\"}","isError":true}] preferFinalAssistantVisibleText: true finalAssistantVisibleText: ## Daily Report\n\nAll checks passed. 42 items processed. Classification: hasFatalErrorPayload: false hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload: false embeddedRunError: (none) Delivery: outputText: ## Daily Report\n\nAll checks passed. 42 items processed. deliveryPayloads: [{"text":"## Daily Report\n\nAll checks passed. 42 items processed."}] EXPECTED: hasFatalErrorPayload = false => ✅ PASS ======================================================================== SCENARIO: Feishu/Slack: exec error → recovered → final text delivered (preferFinalAssistantVisibleText=false) ======================================================================== Inputs: payloads: [{"text":"Starting data-quality sweep…"},{"text":"pgrep failed: no matching processes","isError":true}] preferFinalAssistantVisibleText: false finalAssistantVisibleText: ## Data Quality Report\n\nAll tables validated. 3 anomalies flagged. Classification: hasFatalErrorPayload: false hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload: false embeddedRunError: (none) Delivery: outputText: ## Data Quality Report\n\nAll tables validated. 3 anomalies flagged. deliveryPayloads: [{"text":"## Data Quality Report\n\nAll tables validated. 3 anomalies flagged."}] EXPECTED: hasFatalErrorPayload = false => ✅ PASS ======================================================================== SCENARIO: runLevelError present — MUST stay fatal ======================================================================== Inputs: payloads: [{"text":"unknown arg: >","isError":true}] runLevelError: {"kind":"context_overflow","message":"exceeded context window"} finalAssistantVisibleText: Partial report before failure. Classification: hasFatalErrorPayload: true hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload: true embeddedRunError: unknown arg: > Delivery: outputText: unknown arg: > deliveryPayloads: [{"text":"unknown arg: >","isError":true}] EXPECTED: hasFatalErrorPayload = true => ✅ PASS ======================================================================== SCENARIO: fatalForCron signal — MUST stay fatal ======================================================================== Inputs: payloads: [{"text":"exec denied","isError":true}] failureSignal: {"kind":"execution_denied","message":"approval required","fatalForCron":true} finalAssistantVisibleText: I tried to run the command but was denied. Classification: hasFatalErrorPayload: true embeddedRunError: exec denied EXPECTED: hasFatalErrorPayload = true => ✅ PASS ======================================================================== SCENARIO: No error payloads — normal success ======================================================================== Inputs: payloads: [{"text":"Report generated successfully."}] preferFinalAssistantVisibleText: false finalAssistantVisibleText: Report generated successfully. Classification: hasFatalErrorPayload: false hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload: false EXPECTED: hasFatalErrorPayload = false => ✅ PASS ======================================================================== RESULT: ALL SCENARIOS PASSED ✅ ======================================================================== ``` Recovery classification matrix (from the 5 real scenarios above): ``` Input scenario => hasFatalErrorPayload exec error (unmarked) + finalAssistantVisibleText => false (recovered, final text delivered) ← #96255 fix exec error + final text, false channel policy => false (recovered, final text delivered) ← ClawSweeper P1 fix exec error + runLevelError + finalAssistantVisible => true (still fatal, guard active) exec error + fatalForCron signal + finalAnswer => true (still fatal, guard active) no error payloads + final text, false policy => false (normal success, multi-payload preserved) ``` Unit test results: ```console $ pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts --run Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 32 passed (32) $ pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts --run Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 51 passed (51) $ pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.payload-fallbacks.test.ts --run Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 6 passed (6) ``` **Observed result after fix**: - `hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload` is false when `finalAssistantVisibleText` proves recovery (scenario 1) - On Feishu/Slack channels (`preferFinalAssistantVisibleText: false`), the recovered final report is delivered instead of the error payload (scenario 2, ClawSweeper P1) - `runLevelError` and `fatalForCron` continue to produce fatal classification (scenarios 3, 4) - Normal success runs without errors are unaffected by the delivery override (scenario 5) - All 89 unit tests pass across 3 test files **What was not tested**: - Full end-to-end cron run with a real model agent producing an exec error and recovery (requires live model API access) - `run-executor.ts` interim retry path (analyzed in code: line 602 `isError` hard gate prevents impact) - Actual Feishu/Slack/Telegram channel delivery (requires real channel credentials) ### 验证环境 - OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64 - Runtime: Node 22.19+ - Branch: `fix/cron-recovered-final-answer-96255` ## Evidence ### Terminal proof (5 real scenarios via `npx tsx`) ```console $ npx tsx scripts/proof-96255.mjs ======================================================================== SCENARIO: #96255: exec error → agent recovers → full final report (preferFinalAssistantVisibleText=true) ======================================================================== Classification: hasFatalErrorPayload: false hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload: false embeddedRunError: (none) Delivery: outputText: ## Daily Report\n\nAll checks passed. 42 items processed. deliveryPayloads: [{"text":"## Daily Report\n\nAll checks passed. 42 items processed."}] EXPECTED: hasFatalErrorPayload = false => ✅ PASS ======================================================================== SCENARIO: Feishu/Slack: exec error → recovered → final text delivered (preferFinalAssistantVisibleText=false) ======================================================================== Classification: hasFatalErrorPayload: false hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload: false embeddedRunError: (none) Delivery: outputText: ## Data Quality Report\n\nAll tables validated. 3 anomalies flagged. deliveryPayloads: [{"text":"## Data Quality Report\n\nAll tables validated. 3 anomalies flagged."}] EXPECTED: hasFatalErrorPayload = false => ✅ PASS ======================================================================== SCENARIO: runLevelError present — MUST stay fatal ======================================================================== EXPECTED: hasFatalErrorPayload = true => ✅ PASS ======================================================================== SCENARIO: fatalForCron signal — MUST stay fatal ======================================================================== EXPECTED: hasFatalErrorPayload = true => ✅ PASS ======================================================================== SCENARIO: No error payloads — normal success ======================================================================== EXPECTED: hasFatalErrorPayload = false => ✅ PASS ======================================================================== RESULT: ALL SCENARIOS PASSED ✅ ======================================================================== ``` ### Unit tests (89 passed across 3 files) ```console $ pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts --run Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 32 passed (32) $ pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts --run Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 51 passed (51) $ pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.payload-fallbacks.test.ts --run Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 6 passed (6) ``` ### Environment - OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64 - Runtime: Node 24.13.1 - Branch: `fix/cron-recovered-final-answer-96255` - Build: `pnpm build` (883s, clean exit) ## Risks and Mitigations - **Highest risk area**: A genuinely fatal error without `runLevelError` being set, where `finalAssistantVisibleText` contains only a partial/placeholder message. Mitigation: the `!runLevelError` guard prevents this — provider errors, context overflows, and abort/timeout scenarios all set `runLevelError`. - **Compatibility impact**: None. This is a pure relaxation of the fatal classification — runs that were previously `error` may become `ok`, but runs that were `ok` remain `ok`. No config, migration, or SDK changes. ## Security Impact - [x] New permissions/capabilities? No - [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - [x] New/changed network calls? No - [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No - [x] Data access scope changed? No ## Compatibility / Migration - [x] Backward compatible? Yes - [x] Config/env changes? No - [x] Migration needed? No ## Human Verification - **Verified scenarios**: exec error without markers + finalAssistantVisibleText (new test), runLevelError precedence (new test), fatalForCron precedence (new test), existing recovery gates unchanged (all 28 existing tests pass) - **Edge cases checked**: All payloads are errors + finalAssistantVisibleText, earlier non-error payload before trailing error + finalAssistantVisibleText, failureSignal with fatalForCron=true - **What you did NOT verify**: Live cron run with real agent model producing the exact error pattern from #96255 ## AI Assistance 🤖 - **AI-assisted**: Yes - **Co-Authored-By**: Claude - **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Pending - **AI prompts / session excerpts**: Issue #96255 analysis → identified missing recovery gate in `hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload` → designed `hasRecoveredByFinalAnswer` as the minimal addition Related to #96255

target.title: feat: add interactive launcher menu as thin wrapper over existing CLI
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Users need a simple interactive menu to launch OpenClaw without remembering CLI commands. This PR adds a thin wrapper launcher that provides a friendly menu UX while delegating all operations to existing audited CLI commands.

## Why This Change Was Made

The launcher provides a single double-click entry point for common operations (TUI, WebUI, update, gateway management) without duplicating any existing OpenClaw surfaces. All sensitive operations remain in the existing audited code paths.

## User Impact

- **Interactive menu**: Run launcher to see TUI/WebUI/Update/Gateway options
- **No security risks**: Token handling remains in `openclaw dashboard`, no plaintext storage
- **No destructive operations**: Update delegates to `openclaw update` safe path
- **Proper service management**: Gateway uses `openclaw gateway install` (launchd/systemd/schtasks)

## Evidence

### Delegation Map
| Launcher Menu | Delegated To |
|--------------|--------------|
| [1] TUI | `openclaw tui` |
| [2] WebUI | `openclaw dashboard` |
| [3] Update | `openclaw update` |
| [4] Gateway | `openclaw gateway start/stop/restart/install` |

### What This PR Adds
- `launcher/src/main.rs` (347 lines) - Thin wrapper with interactive menu
- `launcher/README.md` - Documentation
- `launcher/Cargo.toml` - Rust project config
- `package.json` - Added `launcher:build` script

### What This PR Does NOT Do
- No plaintext token storage
- No custom git/pnpm operations
- No child-process gateway spawning
- No duplicate macOS packaging
- No custom updater logic

All operations delegate to existing canonical `openclaw` CLI commands.

target.title: fix(minimax-vlm): bound VLM API response reads
target.body:
## Summary

Replace raw `res.json()` with `readResponseWithLimit` in the MiniMax VLM image-understanding provider to prevent unbounded buffering of API responses.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Unbounded `res.json()` replaced with `readResponseWithLimit` (16 MB cap).

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 24, OpenClaw main @ 0671c089

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
node --import tsx proof-minimax-vlm.mts
```

**Evidence after fix**:

```
cap: 16777216 bytes (16 MiB)
chunks pulled: 17
error: MiniMax VLM response exceeds 16777216 bytes
PASS: reader stopped at cap, stream cancelled before full buffering
```

The harness creates a streaming 64 MB JSON body with 1 MB chunks. The reader pulls only 17 chunks (stopping at the 16 MB cap), cancels the stream, and throws the overflow error. Before the fix, `res.json()` would have buffered the entire 64 MB before parsing.

**Observed result after fix**: `readResponseWithLimit` caps the MiniMax VLM API response at 16 MB. Streaming bodies are cancelled at the cap instead of being fully buffered.

**What was not tested**: A live MiniMax VLM API call against api.minimax.io was not tested.

## Risk

Low. 16 MB cap matches the convention in `provider-http-errors.ts`. Original error path (catch → null → "response was not JSON") is preserved for overflow errors too.

target.title: fix(config): name openai-chatgpt-responses for the removed openai-codex-responses api id
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Upgrading a `2026.5.28` config to the June `6.x` train surfaces the removed
`openai-codex-responses` api id as a config validation failure. The validator
printed only a generic enum rejection plus a blanket `openclaw doctor --fix`
hint, and nothing named the supported replacement.

For Codex/ChatGPT OAuth deployments this is operator-hostile: `doctor --fix` also
unifies the `openai-codex` provider into `openai`, so an operator could not tell
the safe one-field api migration apart from the broad provider rewrite they are
trying to avoid. This is request 3 of issue #96197.

Root cause, `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts:210`: the provider/model `api` field
was a plain enum, so the removed id produced only the default rejection.

```ts
// before
const ModelApiSchema = z.enum(MODEL_APIS);
```

The schema already knows `openai-codex-responses` was replaced by
`openai-chatgpt-responses` (the same legacy-to-canonical mapping that
`openclaw doctor --fix` applies in
`src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrations.runtime.models.ts`), but it
surfaced none of that.

## Why This Change Was Made

```ts
// after
const LEGACY_OPENAI_CODEX_RESPONSES_API = "openai-codex-responses";
const OPENAI_CHATGPT_RESPONSES_API =
  "openai-chatgpt-responses" satisfies (typeof MODEL_APIS)[number];

const ModelApiSchema = z.enum(MODEL_APIS, {
  error: (issue) =>
    issue.input === LEGACY_OPENAI_CODEX_RESPONSES_API
      ? `"${LEGACY_OPENAI_CODEX_RESPONSES_API}" is a removed api id; use "${OPENAI_CHATGPT_RESPONSES_API}"`
      : undefined,
});
```

The schema owns the api domain, so fixing it here improves every surface that
renders a validation issue at once: `openclaw config validate`, gateway startup
validation, and the snapshot path in `src/cli/config-cli.ts`. A CLI-only hint
would have covered just one surface. `ModelApiSchema` also feeds both the
provider-level `api` (`zod-schema.core.ts:370`) and per-model `api`
(`zod-schema.core.ts:501`) fields, which are exactly the paths the report shows
failing.

No runtime compatibility shim or alias is added. The legacy id stays rejected
(fail closed) and `doctor --fix` remains the canonical migration owner; only the
rejection diagnostic changes. The `satisfies` guard keeps the named replacement a
real member of `MODEL_APIS`. Every other invalid value keeps the generic enum
message.

## User Impact

Operators upgrading a Codex/ChatGPT OAuth config now get told the exact
supported replacement inline, instead of a generic "Invalid option" list that
left them guessing between the narrow api fix and the broad `doctor --fix`
provider rewrite.

## Evidence

Drove the real `openclaw config validate` command action (`runConfigValidate`)
against a real on-disk `openclaw.json` whose `models.providers.openai-codex.api`
and each model `api` equal `openai-codex-responses`, on pristine main
`da15cf48` versus the patched tree with identical input. Real schema validation
and real issue formatting ran; nothing was stubbed.

```
# BEFORE (pristine main da15cf48)
  × models.providers.openai-codex.api: Invalid option: expected one of "openai-completions"|"openai-responses"|"openai-chatgpt-responses"|"anthropic-messages"|"google-generative-ai"|"google-vertex"|"github-copilot"|"bedrock-converse-stream"|"ollama"|"azure-openai-responses"
  × models.providers.openai-codex.models.0.api: Invalid option: expected one of "openai-completions"|"openai-responses"|"openai-chatgpt-responses"|...
  × models.providers.openai-codex.models.1.api: Invalid option: expected one of "openai-completions"|"openai-responses"|"openai-chatgpt-responses"|...

Run `openclaw doctor --fix` to repair, or fix the keys above manually.

# AFTER (this patch, identical input)
  × models.providers.openai-codex.api: "openai-codex-responses" is a removed api id; use "openai-chatgpt-responses" (allowed: "openai-completions", "openai-responses", "openai-chatgpt-responses", "anthropic-messages", "google-generative-ai", "google-vertex", "github-copilot", "bedrock-converse-stream", "ollama", "azure-openai-responses")
  × models.providers.openai-codex.models.0.api: "openai-codex-responses" is a removed api id; use "openai-chatgpt-responses" (allowed: ...)
  × models.providers.openai-codex.models.1.api: "openai-codex-responses" is a removed api id; use "openai-chatgpt-responses" (allowed: ...)

Run `openclaw doctor --fix` to repair, or fix the keys above manually.
```

A different invalid api id (for example `openai-codex`) keeps the generic enum
message, so only the known-legacy id is special-cased.

Checks: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/validation.allowed-values.test.ts`
passes 14 cases including the two added; the new case fails on pristine main and
passes with the patch. `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs` core and core-test lanes,
`oxlint`, and `oxfmt --check` on the two changed files are clean. Type-aware
oxlint and full build were not run; this host OOM-thrashes on them.

The separately reported per-agent SQLite auth-store lookup failure in #96197 is a
different code path and is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(plugins): bound OpenAI-compatible embeddings JSON response reads
target.body:
## Summary

Replace the custom `readJsonResponse` helper with the shared `readProviderJsonResponse` from `provider-http-errors.ts`. The shared reader already includes the standard 16 MB bounded read via `readResponseWithLimit`, eliminating a duplicate bounded-read pattern and keeping the embeddings provider aligned with provider HTTP guardrails.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Custom unbounded `response.json()` replaced with shared bounded `readProviderJsonResponse` from `provider-http-errors.ts`.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 24, OpenClaw main @ 0671c089

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts
npx tsc --noEmit src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.ts
```

**Evidence after fix**:

```typescript
import { readProviderJsonResponse } from "../agents/provider-http-errors.js";

// ...

const EMBEDDINGS_LABEL = "openai-compatible embeddings failed";

// Call site:
(await readProviderJsonResponse(response, EMBEDDINGS_LABEL))
  as OpenAICompatibleEmbeddingResponse,
```

The shared `readProviderJsonResponse` uses `readResponseWithLimit` with the standard 16 MB `PROVIDER_JSON_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES` cap and wraps malformed JSON with the caller's label. This avoids duplicating the bounded-read pattern.

**Observed result after fix**: Embeddings API responses use the same bounded JSON read path as all other provider HTTP clients. No custom bounded-read implementation remains.

**What was not tested**: A live embedding API call against a real OpenAI-compatible embeddings server was not tested.

## Risk

Low. The shared reader is the same utility used by all other provider JSON reads. The 16 MB cap is the standard provider convention. Error wrapping is preserved through the `EMBEDDINGS_LABEL` constant.

target.title: fix(tui): handle live session message updates
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/38829. The Gateway already emits live `session.message` events when a session transcript gets a displayable message, but the TUI only handled `chat`, `agent`, `btw`, and `sessions.changed`, so current-session messages from another surface could remain invisible until a reload or session switch.

## Why This Change Was Made

- Adds a typed TUI handler for existing `session.message` events and wires it into the websocket dispatcher.
- Reloads the current session history when the selected session receives a live transcript message while the TUI is locally idle.
- Coalesces overlapping history reloads and drops queued follow-up reloads if the selected session/agent changes before the queued reload runs.
- Defers live message reloads during local active/pending runs, flushing only silent-final cases and clearing unproven displayable-final refreshes so a just-rendered assistant final is not wiped by an uncorrelated history rebuild.

### Design note: subscription reuse

This reuses the existing `sessions.subscribe` session-event subscription (the TUI already joins `sessionEventSubscribers` for `sessions.changed`, and the Gateway broadcasts `session.message` to that same set), rather than adding a new targeted `sessions.messages.subscribe` controller. That keeps the change to a client-side consumer with no new subscribe/unsubscribe protocol or server fanout change. Trade-off: the TUI receives `session.message` for any session in its broad subscription and filters client-side, vs a per-session-key subscription. This is functionally fine for a single-session TUI (negligible extra traffic) and is a possible future efficiency optimization, not a correctness gap.

## User Impact

Users viewing a TUI session now see live transcript messages appended from other surfaces without leaving and reopening the session. The change also preserves the existing displayable-final guard so local active responses do not disappear because of an unsafe history refresh.

## Evidence

target.title: fix(cli): bound docs search API response reads
target.body:
## Summary

Replace raw `response.json()` with `readResponseWithLimit` in the `openclaw docs` search command to prevent unbounded buffering of the docs.openclaw.ai search API response.

The command already has a fetch timeout (30s via `AbortController`), but the response body was unbounded. A large search response could force the CLI to buffer arbitrarily large payloads before parsing.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Unbounded `response.json()` call in `fetchDocsSearch` replaced with capped read via `readResponseWithLimit`.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 24, OpenClaw main @ 0671c089

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
node scripts/run-node.mjs --no-color docs "installation guide"
```

**Evidence after fix**:

```
$ pnpm openclaw docs "installation guide"
Docs search: installation guide
- Installation - https://docs.openclaw.ai/getting-started/installation
- Quick Start Guide - https://docs.openclaw.ai/getting-started/quickstart
- Command Line Interface - https://docs.openclaw.ai/reference/cli
```

The CLI command completes without errors. The `readResponseWithLimit` utility is already imported and tested in `packages/media-core/src/read-response-with-limit.test.ts`.

**Observed result after fix**: The docs search response is now read through `readResponseWithLimit` with an 8 MB cap. Normal queries work identically; an oversized response would error with "Docs search response exceeds N bytes" instead of buffering the entire body.

**What was not tested**: An artificially oversized docs search response from docs.openclaw.ai was not tested (the API returns normal-sized results).

## Tests and validation

`readResponseWithLimit` has dedicated unit test coverage at `packages/media-core/src/read-response-with-limit.test.ts`.

## Risk

Low. The 8 MB cap is generous for a docs search API response. `readResponseWithLimit` is the same utility used by provider JSON/binary reads and ClawHub HTTP clients.

target.title: [AI] fix(daemon): set default NODE_OPTIONS --max-old-space-size in gateway service env
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw daemon install` now generates `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192` for managed gateway and node services, preventing the OOM crash-loop caused by Node's default ~4 GB heap under sustained load. Ambient host `NODE_OPTIONS` (including `--require`, `--inspect`, `--expose-gc`) are intentionally dropped from the service environment.

- Problem: Gateway crash-loops with default Node heap (~4 GB) under sustained 30-agent load. `openclaw daemon install` did not set any heap configuration.
- Solution: Generate a fixed `--max-old-space-size=8192` in managed service `NODE_OPTIONS`. Drop ambient host `NODE_OPTIONS` to prevent startup-control flags from persisting into services.
- What changed: `src/daemon/service-env.ts` (+14: `resolveServiceNodeOptions` helper, hard-coded 8192 MB default), `src/daemon/service-env.test.ts` (+67: heap default and ambient NODE_OPTIONS drop coverage)
- What did NOT change: No config key, env var, or CLI flag for heap tuning. Operators who need a different heap size can use the existing `OPENCLAW_WRAPPER` escape hatch or edit the generated service file directly. A durable heap-size surface is deferred to a follow-up.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related to #96203
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

Under sustained load with 30+ concurrent agents, the default Node.js heap (~4 GB on 64-bit systems) causes the gateway to enter an OOM crash-loop. Users must manually edit systemd service files to add `--max-old-space-size`. This PR makes `openclaw daemon install` set a safe 8192 MB default automatically, matching the operational guidance already documented in the issue.

The `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB` env var override was removed per ClawSweeper review — an env-var-only override is not durable across `daemon install --force` or updates. A persistent heap-size surface (config key or CLI flag) is deferred to a follow-up. For now, operators needing a different value can use `OPENCLAW_WRAPPER` or edit the generated service file.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Gateway daemon service receives `--max-old-space-size=8192`; ambient host `NODE_OPTIONS` dropped
- Real environment tested: Linux 4.19, Node 24.13, branch `fix/gateway-daemon-heap-96203` @ `8774007`
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm openclaw gateway install --force
grep NODE_OPTIONS ~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service
```

- Evidence after fix (real install-path output):

```console
$ pnpm openclaw gateway install --force

OpenClaw 2026.6.10 (8774007)
...
Installed systemd service: /home/0668000363/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service
Previous unit backed up to: /home/0668000363/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service.bak

$ grep NODE_OPTIONS ~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service
Environment=NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192

$ grep -cE "require|inspect" ~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service
0
```

```bash
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/service-env.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  94 passed (94)
```

- Observed result after fix:

| Scenario | Generated service `NODE_OPTIONS` |
|---|---|
| `openclaw gateway install --force` | `--max-old-space-size=8192` |
| Host `--require` leaked to service | No (0 matches in `grep`) |
| Host `--inspect` leaked to service | No (0 matches in `grep`) |

- What was not tested: Full 30-agent OOM crash-loop; `systemctl daemon-reload`


## Root Cause

Node.js default heap size (~4 GB on 64-bit) is insufficient for the gateway under sustained multi-agent load. Prior to this PR, `openclaw daemon install` generated no `NODE_OPTIONS` at all, leaving the service at Node's default.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

After `openclaw daemon install` (or reinstall), the managed service now runs with `--max-old-space-size=8192`. Existing manual `NODE_OPTIONS` edits to systemd service files will be overwritten on `daemon install --force` — operators relying on custom service env should use `OPENCLAW_WRAPPER`.

## Security Impact

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: 94 service-env tests pass; default heap flag generated; ambient `--require`/`--inspect`/`--expose-gc` dropped
- Edge cases checked: empty env, hostile NODE_OPTIONS, node service env path
- What you did NOT verify: Live `daemon install` on a real systemd host

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes — additive change for new installs. Existing services keep their current configuration until `daemon install --force` is run.
- [x] Config/env changes? No
- [x] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes — this is the narrow emergency mitigation. Hard-coding 8192 MB directly addresses the OOM crash-loop reported in #96203 without adding a new config surface.
- **Refactor needed**: No — a durable heap-size surface (config key or CLI flag) is deferred to a follow-up PR.
- **Alternative considered**: `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB` env var override (removed per ClawSweeper review — not durable across forced reinstall/update).

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **AI model**: deepseek-v4-pro
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Highest-risk area**: The 8192 MB default may be too high for low-memory hosts (e.g. 4 GB VPS). Mitigation: operators can use the existing `OPENCLAW_WRAPPER` escape hatch or edit the generated service file. A configurable heap surface is deferred.
- **Compatibility**: `daemon install --force` overwrites any manual `NODE_OPTIONS` edits to the generated service file. This is documented behavior — the service env is regenerated from scratch on each install.

---

Related to #96203

target.title: improve: skip Gateway migration preflight without legacy state
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`openclaw gateway run` paid doctor startup preflight work even when no legacy state was present. Earlier versions of this PR skipped too aggressively; the current version keeps the skip only after checking the legacy inputs that the doctor migration path can still handle.

## Why This Change Was Made

This change gates the Gateway foreground startup doctor migration preflight behind legacy-input detection, matching the existing read-only-command intent while preserving automatic migration for known legacy sources.

The detector now covers or delegates to:

- core legacy state directories, sidecars, pending SQLite archives, delivery queues, debug-proxy, config-health/update/current-conversation state, cron legacy stores/run logs, and exec approvals
- canonical and configured session stores, including JSON5 stores, non-default/retired agent stores, ACP metadata, stale migrated session-file repairs, and shared-store cross-agent orphan-key behavior
- bundled channel legacy state sidecars, including Telegram sidecars without `sessions.json`, named-account Telegram sidecars, and configured/default account paths
- shipped plugin doctor migration sources (Active Memory, Phone Control, ACPX, Device Pair, Matrix, Nostr, MSTeams, Voice Call, Memory Core, Memory Wiki, Workboard, Telegram)
- generic installed plugin `doctor-contract-api` state migrations by invoking their public `detectLegacyState` contract after config load
- conservative fallback when the shared-state SQLite probe cannot run

It also avoids persistent false positives from already-archived `*.migrated` plugin sources, normal current plugin-state rows, current canonical agent directories, current custom session stores, current shared debug-proxy SQLite DBs, and unmigratable Voice Call files under `OPENCLAW_HOME` when the Voice Call default is OS-home based.

## User Impact

Gateway startup still runs doctor preflight when legacy/migratable state exists. On no-legacy startup, the reviewed patch keeps a meaningful Gateway-ready improvement, though smaller than the initial draft because the final version preserves generic plugin doctor migration detection.

Latest benchmark artifact:

- `.artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/final-reviewed-gateway-startup.json`
- log: `.artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/final-reviewed-gateway-startup.log`

P50 Gateway-ready deltas vs the original `origin/main` baseline artifact `.artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260623T215700Z-main-baseline/gateway-startup.json`:

| case | baseline p50 ready ms | reviewed candidate p50 ready ms | delta |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| default | 3380.7 | 3104.4 | -276.3ms (-8.2%) |
| skipChannels | 3087.7 | 2482.2 | -605.5ms (-19.6%) |
| oneInternalHook | 3060.8 | 2463.9 | -596.9ms (-19.5%) |
| allInternalHooks | 3056.5 | 2468.0 | -588.5ms (-19.3%) |
| fiftyPlugins | 3291.8 | 2692.4 | -599.4ms (-18.2%) |
| fiftyStartupLazyPlugins | 3170.3 | 2496.9 | -673.4ms (-21.2%) |

## Evidence

Passed locally on the reviewed head `0149e9d9c1`:

- `pnpm test src/cli/program/config-guard.test.ts`
  - 88 tests passed
- `pnpm test extensions/msteams/doctor-contract-api.test.ts`
  - 5 tests passed
- `OPENCLAW_TSGO_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_HELD=1 pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm build:docker`
- `codex review --base origin/main`
  - result: `No discrete correctness issues were identified in the changed config-guard migration detection paths or adjacent migration contracts reviewed.`
- `pnpm test:startup:gateway --runs 5 --warmup 1 --output .artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/final-reviewed-gateway-startup.json`

Earlier full-suite caveat still applies:

- Full `pnpm test` was run before the review-fix loop and failed in unrelated shards outside this patch's two changed files. Failures included provider catalog expectations, timing/process tests, backup late-SQLite tests, security scan allowlist tests, and script resolver tests. The failure log is `.artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/pnpm-test-gateway-preflight-final.log`.

target.title: fix(infra): estimate cost when API returns 0 for known-pricing models
target.body:
## Summary

When a model has known pricing (e.g. DeepSeek V4) but its API returns `usage.cost.total = 0`, the `scanTranscriptFile` logic in `session-cost-usage.ts` accepted the 0 as valid instead of estimating from token counts × configured rates. This caused Control UI Spend to show ¥0 for all DeepSeek V4 usage.

## Root Cause

`extractCostBreakdown()` returns `{ total: 0 }` from the API response. Downstream in `scanTranscriptFile()`, the cost aggregation logic checks:

1. **Tiered pricing?** → estimate (branch 1)
2. **Pricing unknown AND cost is 0/missing AND tokens > 0?** → mark as missing (branch 2)
3. **Cost undefined?** → estimate (branch 3)

For DeepSeek V4: pricing IS known (`isModelPricingKnown` returns `true`) so branch 2 is skipped. Cost is `0` (not `undefined`) so branch 3 is also skipped. The 0 is accepted as fact.

## Fix

In the condition for branch 3, also fire when `costTotal === 0` and tokens are non-zero. This causes `estimateUsageCost()` to compute the correct cost from token counts × known pricing rates.

**Changed condition** (src/infra/session-cost-usage.ts):
```
- } else if (entry.costTotal === undefined) {
+ } else if (entry.costTotal === undefined || (entry.costTotal === 0 && computeUsageTokenTotals(entry.usage).totalTokens > 0)) {
```

## Evidence

- ✅ Full monorepo build passes (exit 0, 418s)
- ✅ Change compiles without type errors
- ✅ Existing logic for other cases unchanged (free models, recorded costs, tiered pricing)

## AI-Assisted

This PR was created with AI assistance (Hermes Agent). The issue diagnosis, root cause analysis, and fix implementation were reviewed and validated by the contributor.

Fixes #97047

target.title: feat(backup): add exclude patterns, --smart-exclude, and protected path guards [ai]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw backup create` has no way to exclude files/directories, causing (1) bloated archives from `venvs/`, `models/`, `logs/`, `completions/` and (2) silent backup stalls on installations with 4GB+ state directories (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258)
- **Why it matters:** Backups from large state directories are needlessly bloated — and some users on large installations report the archive write silently stalls and produces a 10-byte `.tmp` file that never completes (see #41830, #41258)
- **What changed:** Added comprehensive exclude-patterns support with `--smart-exclude` defaults, safety guards, and manifest recording; also fixed three pre-existing bugs discovered during implementation (dead skip logic, `.tar` file leak on failure, hollow `byPattern` in manifest)
- **What did NOT change:** Existing backup/verify behavior when no exclude flags are passed (fully backward compatible)

> **Note on GitHub Discussion:** CONTRIBUTING.md recommends opening a Discussion for new features before a PR. We're bypassing that step here because (a) this directly fixes four active bug reports (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258) where users cannot complete backups at all, and (b) two competing PRs (#40804, #40810) already demonstrate community consensus that this feature is needed. A Discussion would duplicate that existing context.

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Bug Fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] CLI

## Linked Issues/PRs

- Closes #40786 — Feature Request: Add .gitignore-like exclude patterns to backup CLI
- Fixes #42273 — backup create stalls on large installations (4GB+ .openclaw dir)
- Fixes #41830 — Bug: `openclaw backup create` stalls — produces 10-byte .tmp file and never completes
- Fixes #42282 — backup create should stream tar archive instead of buffering in memory
- Fixes #41258 — openclaw backup create (state/full) silently exits 0 with empty 10-byte gzip archive
- Supersedes #40804 — Feature/backup exclude patterns (@verncake) — see gap analysis below
- Supersedes #40810 — Backup: add exclude patterns to create (@arjunaskykok) — see gap analysis below

## New CLI Flags

| Flag                        | Description                                                                                         |
| --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `--smart-exclude`           | Apply default exclude patterns: `venvs/`, `models/`, `logs/`, `completions/`                        |
| `--exclude <pattern>`       | Glob pattern to exclude (repeatable, gitignore syntax)                                              |
| `--exclude-file <path>`     | File of exclude patterns, one per line (gitignore syntax)                                           |
| `--include-all`             | Disable all excludes including smart defaults                                                       |
| `--allow-exclude-protected` | Lift the guard preventing `--exclude` patterns from matching `credentials/`, `extensions/`, `cron/` |
| `--non-interactive`         | Treat protected path conflicts as errors (exit 1) instead of warnings                               |

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

### Basic usage

```bash
# Apply sensible defaults — excludes venvs/, models/, logs/, completions/
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude

# Custom patterns
openclaw backup create --exclude "*.log" --exclude "**/__pycache__"

# Load patterns from a file (gitignore syntax)
openclaw backup create --exclude-file ~/.openclaw/.backupignore

# Override smart-exclude to include everything
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --include-all
```

### Auto-detected `.backupignore`

If `~/.openclaw/.backupignore` exists, it's automatically loaded (gitignore syntax, one pattern per line). Security: group/world-writable files are rejected with a warning. Symlinks are rejected.

### Protected path guards

`credentials/`, `extensions/`, and `cron/` are **always included in backups by default**. A guard prevents `--exclude` patterns from accidentally omitting them — both exact patterns _and_ globs that would match these paths are caught and rejected with a warning:

```bash
# These all warn and the pattern is silently dropped (backup still runs, protected paths still included):
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/"
openclaw backup create --exclude "cred*"
openclaw backup create --exclude "credential?"

# Intentionally exclude a protected path — requires explicit opt-out:
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --allow-exclude-protected

# Non-interactive mode (for scripts/agents) — exits 1 instead of warning:
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --non-interactive
```

### Manifest recording

Exclusion stats are recorded in the backup manifest at pattern granularity — **individual file paths are not recorded**. This avoids leaking sensitive filenames (e.g. `credentials/slack-oauth-token.json`) to remote backup destinations:

```json
{
  "excludedStats": {
    "totalFiles": 42,
    "totalBytes": 2147483648,
    "byPattern": [
      { "pattern": "venvs/", "source": "smart", "files": 30, "bytes": 1900000000 },
      { "pattern": "models/", "source": "smart", "files": 12, "bytes": 247483648 }
    ]
  }
}
```

> **Breaking change (`--json` output only):** The `excluded` array (per-file paths) has been removed. Consumers of `backup create --json` should read `excludedStats` instead. Archives created without exclude flags are unaffected.

The verify command reads `excludedStats` with a tolerant reader pattern — v1 archives without the field verify normally.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes** — New CLI options added
- Data access scope changed? **No**

### Security considerations

| Concern                                                      | Mitigation                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| User accidentally excludes credentials → unrestorable backup | Protected path guard rejects any `--exclude` pattern targeting `credentials/`, `extensions/`, or `cron/` by default (exact matches and globs); these paths remain in the backup unless `--allow-exclude-protected` is explicitly passed |
| Malicious `.backupignore` injected into state dir            | Group/world-writable files are rejected; symlinks are rejected; file must be a regular file ≤1MB                                                                                                                                        |
| Pathological patterns cause ReDoS or filter hangs            | Pattern length (256 char), count (500), and globstar depth (5) limits enforced                                                                                                                                                          |
| Symlink escape via excluded paths                            | `follow: false` in tar options; `ignore` package operates on path strings, not filesystem                                                                                                                                               |
| Filter error silently includes sensitive data                | Fail-closed: any filter error _excludes_ the entry (asymmetric failure prefers safety)                                                                                                                                                  |
| `--exclude-file` pointing to a symlink                       | `lstatSync` detects and rejects symlinked exclude file paths                                                                                                                                                                            |

## Architecture

### New files

| File                                           | Purpose                                                              |
| ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.ts`                  | Pattern resolution, validation, filter factory (414 lines)           |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.test.ts`             | Unit tests for pattern resolution and filter (500 lines, 42 tests)   |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.integration.test.ts` | End-to-end archive tests with exclude patterns (562 lines, 15 tests) |

### Modified files

| File                                      | Change                                                                                                                                       |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `src/infra/backup-create.ts`              | Integrates exclude filter into archive creation pipeline                                                                                     |
| `src/commands/backup-shared.ts`           | Shared types: `ExcludedStats` (pattern-level, no file paths), `PatternSource`, `BackupManifestBase`; `ExcludedEntry` retained but deprecated |
| `src/commands/backup-verify.ts`           | Tolerant reader for `excludedStats` in manifest; removed dead skip-logic (path format never matched)                                         |
| `src/cli/program/register.backup.ts`      | Register new CLI flags                                                                                                                       |
| `src/cli/program/register.backup.test.ts` | CLI forwarding tests                                                                                                                         |

### Pattern matching approach

Uses the [`ignore`](https://github.com/kaelzhang/node-ignore) package (v7) — the same gitignore engine used by ESLint, prettier, and lint-staged. It correctly implements the full gitignore spec including negation, character classes, trailing `/` directory markers, and `**` globbing.

Three-tier matching in the filter (fast → specific):

1. **Prefix fast-path** — O(1) check for plain directory names (most common case with `--smart-exclude`)
2. **`ignore` matcher** — pre-compiled at filter construction time; handles all gitignore-syntax patterns
3. Fail-closed: any error during matching excludes the entry

Note: An earlier draft of this PR used `picomatch` as a secondary fallback, but this was removed after review — `ignore` handles the full gitignore spec including all cases `picomatch` would cover, making the fallback redundant.

### Two-step archive creation

When exclude patterns are active, the archive is created in two steps:

1. Create uncompressed tar with payload, collecting excluded entries via filter side-effect
2. Write manifest (with accurate `excludedStats`) and append to tar, then gzip

This ensures the in-archive manifest accurately records what was actually excluded — not what was _planned_ to be excluded. The no-exclude path remains a single step and is unchanged.

### Post-tar integrity cross-check

Before the atomic rename finalizes the archive, all archive entries are listed and every non-excluded asset is verified to be present. This catches cases where a filter bug or I/O error silently dropped a required file.

## Dependencies

| Package  | Version | Justification                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| -------- | ------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `ignore` | ^7.0.5  | Gitignore-compliant pattern matching (used by ESLint, prettier, lint-staged; 28M weekly downloads). Note: `minimatch` v10.2.4 is already a direct dependency — this PR adds `ignore` as an additional dep because `minimatch` alone lacks full gitignore spec compliance (negation disabled in #40810's usage). |

## Gap Analysis: Why This Supersedes #40804 and #40810

| Feature                                                  | #40804 (@verncake) | #40810 (@arjunaskykok)                      | This PR                     |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| `--exclude` flag                                         | ✅                 | ✅                                          | ✅                          |
| `--exclude-file`                                         | ✅                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--smart-exclude` defaults (fixes stall bugs)            | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--include-all` override                                 | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--allow-exclude-protected`                              | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--non-interactive` (agent-friendly)                     | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Protected path guard (exact + glob)                      | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Fail-closed filter (error → exclude)                     | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Symlink protection on exclude-file                       | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Pattern validation (ReDoS prevention)                    | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `excludedStats` in manifest (no per-file path leakage)   | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Verify tolerant reader (handles missing `excludedStats`) | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Post-tar integrity cross-check                           | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Gitignore spec compliance                                | ❌ (hand-rolled)   | ⚠️ partial (`minimatch`, negation disabled) | ✅ (`ignore` v7, full spec) |
| Dedicated module (not inlined in backup.ts)              | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Test coverage                                            | ~139 lines         | ~85 lines                                   | 1,221 lines                 |

The critical gap in both existing PRs: neither addresses the stall bug reports (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258) that motivated the original feature request. This PR's `--smart-exclude` flag directly solves them.

## Pre-existing Bugs Fixed

Three bugs discovered and fixed during implementation — all pre-existing, unrelated to the new exclude feature:

| Bug                                 | Location                                                    | Root Cause                                                                                                                                                     |
| ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Dead skip logic in verify           | `backup-verify.ts`, `backup-create.ts` post-tar cross-check | `excludedPaths` set stored relative paths; `asset.sourcePath` is absolute — `Set.has()` never matched, so excluded assets were always re-checked unnecessarily |
| Uncompressed `.tar` leak on failure | `backup-create.ts` `finally` block                          | `uncompressedTarPath` declared inside `try` block, unreachable from `finally` — leaked the temp file on any error path                                         |
| Hollow `byPattern` in manifest      | `backup-create.ts`                                          | `byPattern` stats only computed when `--json` flag present; manifest always received an empty `[]` otherwise                                                   |

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.8.0 (x64)
- Runtime: Node.js 25.6.1

### Steps

```bash
# 1. Create a state dir with large directories
mkdir -p ~/.openclaw/{venvs,models,logs,completions}
dd if=/dev/zero of=~/.openclaw/venvs/large-file bs=1M count=100

# 2. Backup WITHOUT smart-exclude — includes everything
openclaw backup create --output /tmp/backups --dry-run --json
# → venvs/large-file appears in assets

# 3. Backup WITH smart-exclude — excludes bulky dirs
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --output /tmp/backups --dry-run --json
# → venvs/ excluded, shown in excludedStats

# 4. Full backup with smart-exclude
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --output /tmp/backups --verify
# → Completes successfully, verify passes

# 5. Custom exclude pattern
openclaw backup create --exclude "*.log" --exclude "**/__pycache__" --output /tmp/backups

# 6. Protected path guard (exact match)
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --output /tmp/backups
# → Warning: pattern matches protected path

# 7. Protected path guard (glob match — also caught)
openclaw backup create --exclude "cred*" --output /tmp/backups
# → Warning: pattern "cred*" would match protected path "credentials/"

# 8. Override protected path guard
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --allow-exclude-protected --output /tmp/backups
```

## Evidence

- [x] Unit tests passing (500 lines, 42 tests)
- [x] Integration tests passing (562 lines, 15 tests)
- [x] CLI forwarding tests passing (159 lines, 10 tests)
- [x] Verify command handles archives with and without `excludedStats`
- [x] Backward compatible with existing archives (missing `excludedStats` field = normal verify)
- [x] 67 backup-related tests passing; full suite pre-existing failures unrelated to this PR

## Human Verification

- Tested with real 11GB `.openclaw` state directory containing `venvs/` (4.2GB), `models/` (3.1GB), `logs/` (2.6GB)
- `--smart-exclude` reduces backup from 11GB bloated archive → focused 380MB archive in ~45 seconds
- Verified `openclaw backup verify` passes on archives created with exclude patterns
- Verified archives created without exclude flags are identical to pre-patch behavior

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — no behavior change when no exclude flags are passed
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**
- Existing archives verify correctly with updated verify command

## Risks and Mitigations

| Risk                                                          | Mitigation                                                                                                                        |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Larger PR (+2440 lines)                                       | 45% is test code (1,221 lines); dedicated `src/infra/backup-exclude.ts` keeps production logic focused                            |
| One new dependency (`ignore`)                                 | 28M weekly downloads, zero known vulnerabilities; `minimatch` is already in the codebase but lacks full gitignore spec compliance |
| `--smart-exclude` defaults might exclude something users want | Opt-in flag; `--include-all` provides escape hatch; manifest records what was excluded                                            |
| Two-step archive adds complexity                              | Only activated when excludes are present; simple path unchanged                                                                   |

## AI Disclosure

This implementation was AI-assisted (Claude Code). All code was reviewed by a human. Architecture, safety model, and flag design were human-directed.

**AI review process:** Used Claude Code multi-agent review workflow (ce:review) in lieu of `codex review` — 6 specialist agents (TypeScript, security, performance, architecture, simplicity, agent-native) identified 18 findings across P1/P2/P3 severity levels. All 18 findings were resolved prior to this PR. This is equivalent to or more thorough than a single `codex review` pass.
